events.title: The Boy Who Skipped the Draft
events.scene: You are sixteen-year-old Friedrich Drumpf of Kallstadt, a wine village in the Bavarian Palatinate, and the Kingdom of Bavaria expects three years of your body for its army. One October night you leave a letter for your mother, pocket what little the barber's apprenticeship paid, and walk to the boat. Ahead lies the steamship Eider and a country that does not yet know your name.
events.researchNote: Friedrich Trump (born 1869 in Kallstadt) emigrated to the US in 1885 at age 16, partly to avoid Bavarian military service; he had trained as a barber.

choices.text: Tear up the conscription notice and never look back
choices.outcome: You feel the rope to the old country snap. America or nothing. There is a clean, terrible freedom in it.

choices.text: Promise yourself you will come home rich and respectable
choices.outcome: You whisper it to the dark water: you will be back, and Kallstadt will be sorry it ever doubted you. The vow will cost you later.

choices.text: Lose your nerve at the dock and turn back toward the barracks
choices.outcome: The Eider sails without you. You spend three years drilling in Bavarian mud, and the Drumpf name stays in the Palatinate where it has always been.

events.title: A Chair on the Lower East Side
events.scene: The Eider lands you in New York and the city swallows you whole. You find your sister's household and a barber's chair, snipping the beards of German immigrants for pennies while you learn the only English that matters: prices. You sleep in a crowded room and listen to the rumor that out West, men are getting rich faster than anyone can cut hair.
events.researchNote: Friedrich initially lived with his sister Katharina in NYC and worked as a barber for about six years before heading west in the early 1890s.

choices.text: Hoard every coin in a tin under the floorboard
choices.outcome: Six years of beards and bridegrooms fund a small, hard nest egg. It is not a fortune. It is a beginning.

choices.text: Spend your nights reading about the Western boom
choices.outcome: You learn where the silver, gold, and railroad men are going. You decide to be there before they need a meal and a bed.

choices.text: Accept the chair and a quiet immigrant's life
choices.outcome: You could do worse than honest work. But the rumors of the West keep cutting through the lather like a razor.

choices.text: [Alternate Path] Kallstadt was a wine village — leverage that knowledge and go into brewing
choices.outcome: Kallstadt's wine culture runs in your blood as surely as the Rhine runs through the Palatinate. You find a German-immigrant brewery on the Lower East Side, learn every stage of the trade, and invest your barber savings in a small lager operation. When Prohibition comes, the Drumpf brewing knowledge will be worth more than gold — contraband gold.

events.title: The Volstead Act and the Drumpf Brewery
events.scene: It is January 17, 1920, and the Eighteenth Amendment takes effect at midnight. Every legal brewery in America must close or go dark. You are Friedrich Drumpf — or your son Fred, if the fever took Friedrich early — and the Drumpf brewery is a going concern. You can shut down, store the equipment, and wait out Prohibition like respectable men. Or you can pivot the way Joseph Kennedy and a hundred other immigrant businessmen are pivoting right now: convert the brewery into a bootlegging operation, distribute your lager and your Palatinate wine contacts under the table, and ride Prohibition's artificial scarcity to the kind of fortune that turns an immigrant's grandson into a senator's father. The Kennedys are making the same calculation in Boston this very night.
events.researchNote: Counterfactual: Kallstadt, the Drumpf ancestral village, was a wine- and brewing-culture village in the Palatinate. Had Friedrich trained as a brewer rather than a barber, the Drumpf family could plausibly have followed Joseph Kennedy's Prohibition bootlegging route to dynasty wealth — structurally identical paths from immigrant outsider to political-dynasty capital.

choices.text: Convert the brewery to a bootlegging operation — just like the Kennedys in Boston
choices.outcome: You sell the legitimate equipment and build hidden fermentation cellars. Your Palatinate contacts provide cover for aged German lager and Rhine wine under medical-use permits. The money is spectacular — the same money Joseph Kennedy is making in Boston right now from the same play. The difference is only geography: he will turn his into the Democratic machine; you will turn yours into whatever machine serves the Drumpf line best. The dynasty slot is the same. The nameplate is interchangeable.

choices.text: Stay legal — pivot to 'near beer' and malt extract, keep the operation above board
choices.outcome: The Drumpf name stays clean. The fortune grows slower. When Prohibition ends in 1933, you have a viable legal brewery while your richer, dirtier competitors are explaining their business models to the FBI. The Kennedys will have palaces. You will have a solid German-American brewery and a clear conscience.

choices.text: Sell the brewery and pivot to real estate — the Drumpf line finds its historical track
choices.outcome: The brewing experiment ends. You cash out the equipment and contacts for a tidy sum and pivot the Drumpf capital into Queens real estate — the path history actually took. The bootlegger slot stays occupied by the Kennedys. The builder slot is yours.

events.title: Seattle's Lava Beds
events.scene: You take the train as far as it goes and land in Seattle, in the red-light district they call the Lava Beds. You buy a failing restaurant on Washington Street, the Dairy Restaurant, and you serve food, liquor, and — the records hint — the company of women, to miners and sailors who pay in gold dust. It is profitable. It is not the respectable trade your mother imagined.
events.researchNote: Friedrich ran the Dairy Restaurant in Seattle's Lava Beds district in the early 1890s; historians note it likely offered liquor and possibly served as a brothel.

choices.text: Run the food, the bar, and the rooms upstairs — all of it
choices.outcome: The gold dust piles up faster than you can count it. So does the quiet shame, and the attention of men with badges.

choices.text: Serve only food and drink and keep your hands cleaner
choices.outcome: Less money, fewer enemies. The miners still come, and your tin under the floorboard grows into a strongbox.

choices.text: Trust the wrong partner with the deed
choices.outcome: Your partner sells the building out from under you and vanishes north. The Lava Beds are no place to be broke.

events.title: Gold in the Yukon
events.scene: Word comes down the coast: gold on the Klondike, and a hundred thousand stampeders rushing for it. You do not pan for gold — you sell to the men who do. You open the Arctic Restaurant and Hotel in Bennett, then White Horse, feeding and bedding the stampeders, and the smart money says the real fortune is in the boarding-house, not the creek.
events.researchNote: During the Klondike Gold Rush, Friedrich ran restaurants/hotels (the Arctic) in Bennett and White Horse, profiting from the stampeders rather than prospecting himself.

choices.text: Never touch a pan — sell the stampeders their beds and beef
choices.outcome: The men freeze and starve on the creek; you grow rich serving them. You learn the family's oldest lesson: own the road, not the gold.

choices.text: Cash out the moment the Mounties start enforcing liquor laws
choices.outcome: You sell at the top and slip out clean, your strongbox heavy with Yukon gold. Timing, you decide, is everything.

choices.text: Catch gold fever and stake a claim yourself
choices.outcome: The claim is worthless and the winter nearly kills you. You learn the hard way that fortunes are made on the stampeders, not with them.

events.title: Elizabeth Christ of Kallstadt
events.scene: Rich at last, you risk a trip home to Kallstadt and there you marry Elizabeth Christ, a neighbor's daughter half your age. She is homesick for the Palatinate before the ship even sails; you are dreaming of New York. The name on the American papers now reads Trump, anglicized at some clerk's desk somewhere between Bavaria and Bennett.
events.researchNote: Friedrich married Elizabeth Christ in Kallstadt in 1902; by then the family name was spelled 'Trump' in American records. Elizabeth disliked America and wished to stay in Germany.

choices.text: Promise Elizabeth you will settle the family in Kallstadt forever
choices.outcome: She glows with relief. You mean it when you say it. The Kingdom of Bavaria, however, has not forgotten the boy who skipped its draft.

choices.text: Tell her plainly that the future is in New York
choices.outcome: Elizabeth's smile tightens. She marries you anyway, and carries her homesickness across the Atlantic like a stone in her chest.

events.title: Persona Non Grata
events.scene: You petition to live out your days in Kallstadt, your American gold ready to buy a comfortable Bavarian life. The Kingdom's reply is brutal: you fled conscription and never paid your debt to the crown, and so you are expelled — stripped of citizenship, ordered to leave. A wealthy man, and a man with no country to call home.
events.researchNote: Around 1905 Bavaria expelled Friedrich for evading military service and emigrating without permission; his appeals (including a letter to the Prince Regent) failed, forcing the family back to the US.

choices.text: Swallow your pride and take the family back to New York
choices.outcome: Bavaria casts you out, but New York will take your gold. You set your jaw and book passage for Queens, fortune intact.

choices.text: Burn money and years fighting the expulsion in the courts
choices.outcome: The appeals fail one by one. You bleed the strongbox dry against a king's bureaucracy, and still they show you the border.

choices.text: Refuse to leave and let the fortune wither in legal limbo
choices.outcome: You cling to Kallstadt until the gold runs out and the order stands. The Trump fortune evaporates in a Bavarian courtroom.

events.title: Back to Queens
events.scene: Elizabeth, pregnant and weeping for the village she will never see again, shepherds the family back across the ocean and into the Bronx, then out to Woodhaven, Queens. On October 11, 1905, in this new-old country, your son Fred is born — Frederick Christ Trump, the boy who will pour the family's gold into bricks. The Trumps are New Yorkers now, whether Elizabeth likes it or not.
events.researchNote: Frederick Christ Trump was born October 11, 1905, in the Bronx (the family soon settled in Queens) after the family's forced return to the US; Elizabeth had wanted to remain in Germany.

choices.text: Put the Yukon gold straight into Queens real estate
choices.outcome: You buy vacant lots while they are cheap. Fred will grow up watching land turn into money, and money into more land.

choices.text: Keep the gold liquid and live modestly in Woodhaven
choices.outcome: Elizabeth makes a careful German home far from the village she misses. The strongbox stays full, waiting for a use.

choices.text: Spend lavishly to look like established New Yorkers
choices.outcome: The neighbors are impressed. The strongbox is lighter. Fred learns early that appearance is a kind of currency.

events.title: A German Name in a German War
events.scene: America enters the Great War against the Kaiser, and suddenly your German name is a liability on every Queens block. Neighbors who once tipped their hats now eye you sideways; the papers print hate for anything Prussian. Friedrich, you must decide how the Trumps weather the suspicion of being German in a country at war with Germany.
events.researchNote: Anti-German sentiment surged in the US after it entered WWI in April 1917; the Trumps' German heritage drew suspicion, a pressure the family downplayed for decades (later even claiming Swedish ancestry).

choices.text: Quietly bury the family's German roots
choices.outcome: You speak only English in the street and never mention Kallstadt. The instinct to recast the family's story will outlive you by a century.

choices.text: Refuse to hide who the family is
choices.outcome: You will not apologize for Kallstadt. It costs you customers and goodwill, but Elizabeth holds her head higher for it.

choices.text: Out-patriot the neighbors with flags and war bonds
choices.outcome: No house on the block flies a bigger flag than yours. Suspicion gives way to grudging respect — and a lesson Fred files away.

events.title: The Spanish Flu at the Door
events.scene: On a single afternoon in May 1918, walking with twelve-year-old Fred, you feel suddenly, terribly ill. The influenza scything through the world — fifty million dead before it burns out — has come for you, Friedrich Trump, at forty-nine. Whether it takes you or spares you will decide whose hands shape the family line: a widow and a boy, or the bold old operator himself.
events.researchNote: Friedrich Trump died of the 1918 influenza pandemic on May 30, 1918, at age 49; the assets passed to Elizabeth, who held them in trust and signed for her minor son Fred. The pandemic killed an estimated 50 million worldwide — a true world-event hinge for the family.

choices.text: [Historical] The flu takes Friedrich — he leaves Elizabeth deeds to build on
choices.outcome: Friedrich dies as he did in life's record. Elizabeth folds the deeds and cash into a family company; young Fred will build the outer-borough firm his own way. The seed money survives the man who earned it.

choices.text: [Historical] The flu takes Friedrich — and the nest egg was already spent thin
choices.outcome: Friedrich dies and leaves little but a name and a widow's grit. Fred grows up poor, watching his mother count every cent, vowing he will never be small again.

choices.text: [Counterfactual: no pandemic] Friedrich survives and keeps building himself
choices.outcome: The fever breaks; Friedrich lives. The bold, seedy Klondike operator stays at the helm of the family — and the line will be shaped by his risk appetite, not his cautious son's. One absent world-event has bent the whole hand.

events.title: The Grandfather's Gambit
events.scene: In the world where the flu spared you, you are Friedrich Trump, alive into your fifties with a Klondike fortune and an operator's nerve no schoolteacher ever bred out of you. You build in Queens a decade before your son would have, betting bigger and dirtier than cautious Fred ever will — and you decide whether the Trump name rises early on your gambles, or burns on them. Fred grows up in your long shadow, learning to wait his turn.
events.researchNote: Counterfactual: had Friedrich survived 1918, his documented risk appetite (Lava Beds, Klondike liquor-and-rooms operations) suggests he might have seeded the real-estate empire a generation earlier — or squandered it — and overshadowed Fred's own development of the firm.

choices.text: Build a bold empire a generation early and hand it down
choices.outcome: Friedrich's gambles pay off enormous; the Trump fortune is seeded a generation early and weathers what comes. Any grandchild born to this line starts with a deeper, older war chest than history ever gave Donald.

choices.text: Keep the reins yourself and let Fred chafe in your shadow
choices.outcome: Friedrich never lets go; Fred is a manager, not a master, well into middle age. The firm survives, modestly, but its character is the grandfather's — and the line's ambition is tangled in a rivalry of fathers and sons.

choices.text: Chase one risk too many and squander the Yukon gold
choices.outcome: The old operator's luck finally runs out. A bad speculation swallows the strongbox, and Fred inherits debts instead of deeds. The bold grandfather builds nothing lasting but a cautionary tale.

events.title: Elizabeth Trump & Son
events.scene: You are now young Fred Trump, eighteen and impatient, and your mother Elizabeth signs the contracts you are too young to hold yourself. With her seed money you build your first house in Queens and sell it, then build another. You name the firm Elizabeth Trump & Son — her capital, your ambition — and you discover you can pour a foundation faster than any builder in the borough.
events.researchNote: Fred Trump began building homes in Queens in the early 1920s; because he was a minor, his mother Elizabeth signed legal documents, and the firm was named 'E. Trump & Son'.

choices.text: Plow every dollar of profit back into the next house
choices.outcome: House becomes two becomes a block. Fred learns the developer's catechism: never stop building, never let the money sit still.

choices.text: Build steadily and bank healthy reserves
choices.outcome: Fred's name spreads quietly through Queens as a man who finishes what he starts and pays his bills. The firm is sound.

choices.text: Borrow heavily to build faster than the market wants
choices.outcome: Fred stacks houses on borrowed money and crossed fingers. It works — for now — but the ledger is stretched thin as paper.

events.title: The Crash and the Self-Serve Grocery
events.scene: Wall Street collapses and the building trade dies overnight; no one in Queens is buying houses in 1929. You are Fred Trump with a half-built business and no buyers, so you pivot — opening a self-service grocery store, the Trump Market, to ride out the Depression while every rival developer goes under. Survival now matters more than ambition.
events.researchNote: When the Depression halted homebuilding, Fred Trump ran a self-service grocery store (Trump Market) in Woodhaven, Queens, to stay solvent until housing demand returned.

choices.text: Run the grocery and keep your land while rivals go broke
choices.outcome: Cans of beans keep the lights on while the borough starves. When building returns, Fred owns land the bankrupt left behind.

choices.text: Use the grocery cash to buy up distressed mortgages cheap
choices.outcome: Fred picks bargains off the bones of the crash — a foreclosed mortgage-servicing business, cheap lots, ruined men's collateral. The fortune compounds in the wreckage.

choices.text: Let the overleveraged firm collapse with the market
choices.outcome: The borrowed houses are repossessed and Elizabeth Trump & Son dissolves into debt. Fred joins the breadlines he swore he would never see.

events.title: The FHA Gravy Train
events.scene: Roosevelt's New Deal creates the Federal Housing Administration, and suddenly Washington will insure mortgages and underwrite mass housing for the working class. You are Fred Trump, and you see it instantly: government-backed money to build thousands of homes at guaranteed margins. The FHA does not just save your business — it makes you, and it teaches you that the surest profit flows from the public trough.
events.researchNote: Fred Trump built much of his fortune on FHA-insured mortgages and federal housing programs from the 1930s onward, becoming one of the most prolific FHA-backed builders in Brooklyn and Queens.

choices.text: Cultivate the FHA officials and milk every program
choices.outcome: Fred builds Brooklyn and Queens by the thousand-unit on Uncle Sam's dime. He learns to court politicians like clients. The Trump dynasty's true capital is born here.

choices.text: Use the FHA modestly and keep your hands clean of the politics
choices.outcome: Fred builds solid, profitable housing without overreaching the program. A comfortable fortune, but not yet a dynasty's war chest.

choices.text: Distrust the federal money and sit the program out
choices.outcome: While rivals build empires on FHA paper, Fred scrapes by as a small landlord of aging walk-ups. The big money sails past the Trump name.

events.title: Mary Anne MacLeod of the Isle of Lewis
events.scene: At a dance you are Fred Trump and you meet Mary Anne MacLeod, a Scottish maid from the windswept Isle of Lewis who crossed the ocean in 1930 looking for work and a richer life. She is beautiful, ambitious, and as much an immigrant climber as your own father was. You marry her in 1936, and together you will raise a brood in Queens — including, one day, a boy named Donald.
events.researchNote: Fred Trump married Mary Anne MacLeod, a Scottish immigrant from the Isle of Lewis who arrived in the US in 1930, in January 1936; they had five children, of whom Donald was the fourth.

choices.text: Marry Mary Anne and plan a dynasty of Trump children
choices.outcome: Two immigrant bloodlines — Bavarian and Hebridean — fuse in a Queens row house. The children come quickly, and Fred dreams of heirs to the firm.

choices.text: Marry quietly and keep the household frugal
choices.outcome: Mary Anne runs a thrifty, churchgoing home, never quite shaking the memory of Lewis poverty. The Trump children grow up watched and disciplined.

events.title: Building for the War Effort
events.scene: World War II is on, and you are Fred Trump with the contacts and the FHA expertise to win federal contracts for war-worker and Navy housing in Norfolk, Virginia, and along the Eastern seaboard. While other men ship out, you build the barracks and apartments that house the war machine, banking government money by the project. The empire compounds even as the world burns.
events.researchNote: During WWII Fred Trump built federally financed housing for naval and war workers (notably near Norfolk, Virginia), expanding his fortune through wartime government contracts.

choices.text: Win every Navy housing contract you can grab
choices.outcome: Fred ends the war richer than his father ever dreamed, with a fortune, a Cadillac, and politicians on speed-dial. Whatever child comes next will be born into real money.

choices.text: Take a fair share of contracts and stay below the radar
choices.outcome: Fred profits handsomely without the scrutiny that follows the biggest fish. The Trump household enters 1946 comfortably, undeniably rich.

events.title: The German-Immigrant Tenement
events.scene: You are Fred Trump in the version of this story where the gravy train never came — a small landlord scraping rents from aging walk-ups in a German-immigrant corner of Queens. Mary Anne mends clothes; the children share rooms; there is no Cadillac and no FHA fortune. Whatever child is born into this house will know stoops and public school, not gold elevators.
events.researchNote: Counterfactual branch: had Fred missed the FHA/wartime housing boom, the family would have remained modest landlords rather than millionaire developers — Donald's actual childhood was affluent and suburban (Jamaica Estates).

choices.text: Resolve that any child of yours will have to earn it
choices.outcome: Fred fixes his own boilers and counts his own rents. The child to come will inherit grit and a German surname, but no empire to coast on.

choices.text: Bet the family's last savings on one risky building
choices.outcome: Fred rolls the dice to climb back up. Win or lose, the house stays poor through 1946, and any child arrives into striving, not splendor.

events.title: The Dynasty That Never Was
events.scene: And here the thread of the family simply frays and breaks. Whether Friedrich never left Kallstadt, or died stranded and broke in Bavaria, or Fred's overborrowed firm was swallowed whole by the Depression — the Trumps never become builders, never plant a fortune in Queens soil. There will be no Cadillac, no FHA empire, and no June morning in 1946. History closes over the family before it ever truly begins.
events.researchNote: Terminal counterfactual: each real failure point (draft, expulsion, the 1918 flu, the Depression) could plausibly have ended the family's rise — without Fred's fortune, the dynasty has no foundation.

choices.text: Let the Drumpf line vanish back into history
choices.outcome: The name dissolves into the great anonymous tide of immigrants who came, struggled, and left no dynasty behind. There is no Donald. The game ends at its prologue.

choices.text: Rage at the fortune that slipped away
choices.outcome: Friedrich, or Fred, dies bitter and broke, cursing the country that took everything and gave nothing back. The Trump dynasty is stillborn. There is no Donald.

events.title: The Boy Who Served the King
events.scene: In this telling you do not walk to the boat. You report to the Bavarian barracks, take the kaiser's coat, and serve your three years drilling in the Palatinate mud. When you muster out, the Drumpf name has earned something it never had in America: a soldier's standing in a kingdom that grooms its veterans for the next war. Kallstadt keeps its wine, its conscripts, and now its rising military family.
events.researchNote: Counterfactual divergence from the documented draft-dodging: in reality Friedrich Trump fled Bavaria in 1885 specifically to avoid the three-year conscription. Here he serves instead, anchoring the family inside Imperial Germany's military caste on the eve of the World Wars.

choices.text: Angle for an officer's commission and a name in the regiment
choices.outcome: You climb from conscript to junior officer, and the Drumpfs of Kallstadt become a family that sends its sons to the army first and the vineyard second. The kingdom remembers loyal blood when the great wars come.

choices.text: Serve your term, then go home to the barber's chair and the vines
choices.outcome: You do your duty and return to Kallstadt a respectable veteran. No empire, no exile — just a German family putting down deeper German roots, with sons who will be of fighting age when the century turns dark.

choices.text: Lose your nerve mid-service and desert across the border
choices.outcome: Half a soldier and half a fugitive, you bolt — too late to build the American fortune, too marked to stay. The line frays in a no-man's-land between two countries.

events.title: Verdun and the Family Colors
events.scene: The Great War grinds across Europe and the Drumpfs of Kallstadt are no bystanders — your sons wear field-grey, and the family's standing rises with every dispatch from the front. Germany loses in 1918, and the humiliation of Versailles lands like a wound on a household that gave its blood for the kaiser. In the bitter postwar Palatinate, a soldier's family learns to nurse a grievance into a creed.
events.researchNote: Imperial Germany's defeat in 1918 and the punitive Treaty of Versailles fed the 'stab-in-the-back' myth and the revanchism that fascist movements weaponized in the 1920s-30s. A veteran military family in the Rhineland-Palatinate would have sat squarely in that current.

choices.text: Let the loss harden into bitterness and a hunger for revenge
choices.outcome: Around the family table, defeat becomes betrayal and Versailles becomes a debt to be collected. The next movement that promises to make Germany great again will find willing recruits under your roof.

choices.text: Mourn the dead and try to rebuild a normal life
choices.outcome: You bury sons and try to be done with war. But in a Weimar of hunger and street-fighting, a respectable military name is a prize the radicals keep circling back to claim.

events.title: The Party and the Family Name
events.scene: Hitler takes the chancellorship in January 1933, and the new order comes courting families exactly like yours — old soldiers, wounded pride, German blood and a grudge against Versailles. You are the head of the Drumpf household, and the Party offers your veterans rank, your name prestige, and your sons a future in a Reich that swears it will rule for a thousand years. To refuse is to be nobody; to join is to ride the tide.
events.researchNote: The Nazi regime actively recruited from veterans' associations and the conservative-nationalist milieu after 1933. This is the chilling pivot of the branch: the same grievance machinery that radicalized real interwar Germany, applied to a fictional Trump-line that never emigrated.

choices.text: Bind the family to the Party and rise with the Reich
choices.outcome: Your sons take their oaths and the Drumpf name is stamped into the machinery of the new state. The family fortune and standing swell on conquest and confiscation. There is no shame left in the house — only ambition, dressed in a uniform.

choices.text: Join cynically — for contracts and rank, not the creed
choices.outcome: You tell yourself you are using them, not the other way around. The distinction will not survive what the family is asked to do. Either way the Drumpfs prosper, and the line is bound to the swastika.

choices.text: Refuse the Party and accept the family's ruin
choices.outcome: You will not give your sons to it. The Reich does not forgive a prominent family that says no — the contracts dry up, the visits from men in leather coats begin, and the Drumpf line is hounded into silence and exile.

events.title: The Reich Across the Atlantic
events.scene: In this darkest branch the Axis does not lose. The war turns, the Reich prevails, and by the mid-1940s a defeated United States is governed under occupation — a Nazi-controlled America, its institutions hollowed and remade. Your family, loyal Party men of long standing, are sent across the Atlantic as part of the conquering order, carrying the Drumpf name into a New York that no longer answers to itself.
events.researchNote: Pure alternate-history (in the tradition of 'The Man in the High Castle'): there was no Axis victory and no Nazi occupation of the US. This counterfactual exists to make the branch's thesis unmistakable — that the stay-in-Germany path converges back toward American power, but as tyranny rather than enterprise.

choices.text: Take a governor's role in the occupied American order
choices.outcome: The family arrives in New York not as huddled immigrants but as overlords — administrators of a conquered country, with confiscated fortunes and a borough's worth of seized property. Any child born to this line inherits power built on terror, and a name that means fear.

choices.text: Stay out of the killing and just run the new economy
choices.outcome: You tell yourself the administrator's lie — that someone competent must run things, that you are sparing the city worse. The fortune is real, the comfort is real, and the blood underneath it never washes out. A child of this house is born advantaged and damned.

choices.text: Sicken of it and try to defect to the underground
choices.outcome: You came as a conqueror and find you cannot stomach the cellars and the lists. You slip your own family's apparatus and run for the resistance — wealth gone, name poisoned, hunted by the order that made you. A hard, late conscience in a ruined world.

events.title: Anchored in the Pacific
events.scene: Rich from the Lava Beds and the Klondike, you make a different choice than history records: you never sail east to chase a Bavarian homecoming, and you never resettle in Queens. You and Elisabeth plant the family fortune on the Pacific slope — Seattle timber and waterfront, then California land — and the Trumps become a West Coast house, builders and boosters of a booming young coast that has never heard of New York real estate.
events.researchNote: Friedrich genuinely made his fortune in Seattle and the Yukon (1891-1901) and owned Pacific Northwest property before the family ultimately settled back East. This branch keeps him anchored in his real Pacific period instead of returning to NYC, founding a West-Coast dynasty.

choices.text: Pour the Yukon gold into California land and ride the boom
choices.outcome: You buy orange groves and town lots ahead of the boosters and the railroads, and the Trump name becomes a power on the Pacific coast a full generation before it ever would in New York. A child of this house is born a Western scion.

choices.text: Stay in Seattle as a solid timber-and-waterfront man
choices.outcome: You build a comfortable, respectable Pacific Northwest fortune — lumber, docks, a fine house on a Seattle hill. No empire, but an undeniable regional dynasty rooted in the Cascades, not the Atlantic.

choices.text: Speculate too hard on coast land and a railroad that stalls
choices.outcome: You bet on a rail line and a townsite that never quite arrive, and the strongbox bleeds. The family stays West, but as strivers clawing back, not coast barons — a leaner Pacific hand to pass down.

events.title: Sons of the Pacific
events.scene: On the West Coast the Trump line grows up under different skies — orange light and ocean fog instead of Queens brick. Fred (or his Pacific counterpart) learns the family trade not in outer-borough walk-ups but in California subdivisions and Seattle waterfront, building the suburbs of a coast that is inventing the American future. The fortune compounds far from New York, in a dynasty that thinks of the Atlantic as the old world.
events.researchNote: In reality Fred Trump built in Brooklyn and Queens on FHA financing. This West-Coast counterfactual relocates that homebuilding career to California/Pacific growth markets, which boomed enormously in the same era — plausible parallel infrastructure, different geography.

choices.text: Build the booming Pacific suburbs and bank federal housing money
choices.outcome: The family rides the same New Deal and wartime housing money history gave the real Fred — but pours it into Los Angeles and the Bay instead of Brooklyn. The Trump name becomes Pacific royalty, and the next child is born to a coast empire.

choices.text: Build steadily and keep the dynasty regional and grounded
choices.outcome: A solid, prosperous West Coast builder's family — country clubs and citrus, not skyscrapers. The child to come is a comfortable Western heir, raised believing the center of the world is the Pacific, not the Hudson.

events.title: The Calling in the Congregation
events.scene: Kallstadt was a Lutheran village, and Elisabeth carries that faith across the ocean like a relic. In this telling, instead of pouring the Yukon gold only into bricks, the family pours it into the pulpit — bankrolling a struggling immigrant Lutheran congregation, then a second, then a circuit of them, with the Trump name on every cornerstone. You discover that in a young, hungry country, a man who builds churches commands a different and deeper kind of loyalty than a man who only builds houses.
events.researchNote: Kallstadt was historically Lutheran, and German-Lutheran immigrant churches were a real institutional force in early-20th-century North America. The genuine historical contingency: Lutheran pietism shared revivalist DNA with the Baptist/Methodist strains that actually came to dominate American evangelicalism — a different denomination could plausibly have ridden the same populist-religious current.

choices.text: Bankroll a circuit of churches and make the name holy
choices.outcome: The strongbox empties into hymnals and steeples, but something more valuable fills up: a congregation that calls your family blessed. The Trumps stop being mere builders and start being shepherds — and shepherds are followed, not just hired.

choices.text: Build one mission and keep faith a private family matter
choices.outcome: You raise one modest mission church and keep your devotion close to the chest. The family is known as pious and respectable — a softer foundation, but a religious dynasty in seed, waiting for a louder generation.

choices.text: Decide the gold belongs in real estate, not the collection plate
choices.outcome: You tip your hat to the pastor and keep your wallet shut. The family stays in bricks and mortar — the church branch closes here, and history's secular road reopens.

events.title: Tents, Radio, and the Roaring Word
events.scene: By the 1920s the family church-circuit has grown into something the old Kallstadt pastors would not recognize: tent revivals, a radio hour, an evangelist's gift for filling a hall and a collection basket. You are the heir now, and you see what the Baptists and the revivalists are building across the country — and you mean to build it bigger, in the German-Lutheran key, fusing pulpit and showmanship into a movement with the Trump name at its altar.
events.researchNote: The 1920s saw the rise of mass-media evangelism (Aimee Semple McPherson, radio preachers, tent crusades). The counterfactual conceit: a Lutheran-rooted family adopting the same revivalist mass-movement techniques that, in reality, belonged to other Protestant strains — a plausible alternate denominational winner of the populist-religious moment.

choices.text: Fuse pulpit and showmanship into a national movement
choices.outcome: The tents fill, the radio carries the name into a million kitchens, and the offering plates come back heavy. The family becomes a religious power in the land — and the heir learns that a crowd that believes in you will give you anything, including its vote.

choices.text: Preach a stern, moralizing gospel of order and obedience
choices.outcome: Your movement is built on thou-shalt-not and a strongman's certainty that someone must enforce the commandments. It draws the frightened and the furious in droves. The family's power now wears a robe, and it does not ask permission.

choices.text: Refuse the spectacle and tend a real, humble flock
choices.outcome: You choose the cure of souls over the cult of personality. The family stays a respected religious house, beloved and modest — a dynasty of conscience rather than a machine of power. The road to the throne forks away here.

events.title: Born to the Pulpit
events.scene: In the branch where the family answered a different calling, the child is born not heir to a building firm but heir to a movement of believers. Donald arrives a scion of the Trump evangelical dynasty — cradled in a congregation that numbers in the hundreds of thousands, taught from the crib that the family was chosen, and that the crowd's love is the surest currency on earth. The prologue ends with a different kind of birthright: not bricks, but faith bent toward power.
events.researchNote: Counterfactual capstone to the religious branch, parallel to the secular 'born to empire' and the dystopian 'son of the occupation' outcomes. The thesis: a religious-populist route arriving at the same destination — inherited mass loyalty convertible into political power.

choices.text: Born the chosen heir of a great revival movement
choices.outcome: Donald is born into a kingdom of believers, raised to mistake adoration for proof and a full collection plate for a mandate. The family fortune is measured in followers, and followers, he will learn, can be marched to the polls. (Requires evangelical_scion + faith_to_power.)

choices.text: Born heir to a stern moral-authoritarian ministry
choices.outcome: Donald arrives into a house that preaches obedience and punishment in equal measure, where the family's word is law and dissent is sin. He is taught that to lead is to command, and that righteousness and ruling are the same thing. (Requires moral_authoritarian.)

events.title: A Son of the Occupation
events.scene: In the branch where the family stayed, served, and rode the Reich across the ocean, the child is not born into Queens striving but into a conquered New York the Drumpfs help administer. Donald arrives a scion of a Nazi house in a Nazi-ruled America — surrounded by confiscated wealth, uniformed loyalty, and a name that opens every door because it makes people afraid. The prologue ends not with an immigrant's hope but with an heir to occupation.
events.researchNote: Counterfactual capstone to the stay-in-Germany branch. There was no Axis victory; this dystopian birth exists to show the path converging back to American power as inherited tyranny, the dark mirror of the historical 'born advantaged' outcome.

choices.text: Born heir to the occupied order, raised to inherit power
choices.outcome: Donald is born into a war chest of seized fortunes and a country that does not get a vote. He will be raised to believe that power is the family's right and fear its native language. The hand is dealt, and it is the most loaded — and most damning — in the deck. (Requires arrived_as_nazi.)

choices.text: Born into a Party house quietly sickening of itself
choices.outcome: The fortune is overwhelming, but somewhere in the house a conscience is fraying. Donald is born advantaged beyond anything history gave him, into wealth that everyone around him has learned not to look at too closely. (Requires arrived_as_nazi without ruling the occupation.)

events.title: June 14, 1946
events.scene: Mary Anne goes into labor at Jamaica Hospital, Queens, on June 14, 1946, and Fred Trump paces the corridor. A fourth child, a son, red-faced and bawling, is laid in her arms: Donald John Trump. The world he opens his eyes on — gilded or grinding — is the one the family has spent sixty years and three generations building. The prologue is over. The hand is dealt.
events.researchNote: Donald John Trump was born June 14, 1946, at Jamaica Hospital in Queens, the fourth of Fred and Mary Anne Trump's five children; the family was by then wealthy, living in Jamaica Estates.

choices.text: [If the family went West] Born a West Coast scion, not a Queens one
choices.outcome: Donald opens his eyes not on Jamaica Hospital but on the Pacific coast — heir to a Western land fortune, raised under orange light to believe the center of America is the ocean, not the Hudson. The same birthright of winning, dealt from a different deck. (Requires west_coast_dynasty.)

choices.text: [If the dynasty was built] Born into the {surname} empire
choices.outcome: Donald arrives heir to an FHA fortune, a connected father, and a borough full of Trump bricks. He will be raised to believe winning is the family's birthright. (Requires dynasty_capital from Fred's choices.)

choices.text: [If Fred built a modest fortune] Born into solid comfort
choices.outcome: Donald arrives into a comfortable, striving household with a successful builder for a father — advantaged, if not yet imperial. (Requires real_capital without the full dynasty war chest.)

choices.text: [If the fortune was lost] Born middle-class in the immigrant warren
choices.outcome: Donald arrives into a German-immigrant tenement, public-school bound, with no inherited empire to coast on. Whatever he becomes, he will have to build from nothing. (The bootstrap game.)
